production_metadata.audio_contract: cached AI audio first with browser speech fallback; audio fields optional

subject: English
skill: Vocabulary, Context Clues, and Figurative Language

lesson.story_mission: Enter the Word Meaning Workshop, where readers unlock unfamiliar words, precise shades, idioms, adages, and proverbs for a school storytelling festival.
lesson.objective: I can determine word meaning using context clues, roots and affixes, synonyms, antonyms, shades of meaning, figurative language, adages, and proverbs.
lesson.concept_explanation: Strong readers do not skip unfamiliar words. They use surrounding sentences, meaningful word parts, word relationships, precise shades, and nonliteral language to choose a meaning that fits the text.
lesson.common_misconception: Readers may ignore context clues, read idioms literally, mix up adages and proverbs, or choose a synonym with the wrong strength.
lesson.real_world_connection: Vocabulary skills help students understand novels, articles, speeches, science texts, directions, and everyday sayings.
lesson.lesson_summary: Use context first, check roots and affixes, compare word relationships and shades, and decide whether a phrase is literal or figurative.
lesson.mini_lesson: Read around the word or phrase. Ask what clues the author gives. Then test the meaning in the sentence and choose the most precise answer.

audio.text: context clues, root, prefix, suffix, synonym, antonym, shade of meaning, figurative language, idiom, adage, proverb
audio.label: Listen
audio.student_prompt: Listen, then repeat the vocabulary words.

worked_examples.prompt: In the sentence, what does scarce mean? "During the drought, fresh water was scarce, so families saved every drop."
hints: Read the full sentence or expression before choosing.
Look for clue words, word parts, relationships, or the situation.
Check whether the meaning is literal, figurative, or a stronger/weaker shade.
worked_examples.explanation: The drought and saving every drop show that scarce means hard to find.
worked_examples.feedback: Good vocabulary thinking. You used context, word parts, relationships, or figurative meaning instead of guessing.

question_audio.text: In the sentence, what does scarce mean? "During the drought, fresh water was scarce, so families saved every drop." Choices: hard to find, easy to waste, brightly colored.

audio.text: During the drought, fresh water was scarce, so families saved every drop.
audio.label: Listen
audio.student_prompt: Listen to the word, sentence, idiom, adage, or proverb before you answer.

context_clue_ignored.description: Student chooses a familiar or literal meaning without using sentence clues.
context_clue_ignored.remediation: Have the student underline definition, example, contrast, or cause-effect clues and replace the word with the inferred meaning.

literal_vs_figurative_confusion.description: Student interprets idioms, similes, or metaphors as literal pictures.
literal_vs_figurative_confusion.remediation: Ask what the phrase cannot literally mean in context, then restate the intended meaning.

adage_proverb_confusion.description: Student treats a traditional saying as a one-time event instead of a general lesson.
adage_proverb_confusion.remediation: Identify the lesson the saying teaches and apply it to a new situation.

shade_of_meaning_confusion.description: Student picks a related word with the wrong intensity, formality, or precision.
shade_of_meaning_confusion.remediation: Place choices on a word scale and discuss which word best fits the exact context.

questions.prompt: Which meaning fits the proverb "Don’t count your chickens before they hatch"?
questions.correct_answer: Do not assume success before it happens.
hints: Read the full sentence or expression before choosing.
Look for clue words, word parts, relationships, or the situation.
Check whether the meaning is literal, figurative, or a stronger/weaker shade.
questions.explanation: The proverb warns against planning as if something is certain before it is.
questions.feedback: Good vocabulary thinking. You used context, word parts, relationships, or figurative meaning instead of guessing.
